passages.explanation: Dorian wakes late in luxurious surroundings, already distanced from the previous night's trauma. The elaborate details of his lifestyle, the Sevres china, the silver toilet-set, the casual debts, establish him as a man cushioned by wealth from consequence. His hesitation over Lord Henry's letter is significant: he instinctively avoids anything that might disturb his carefully maintained surface.

The morning routine washes away guilt with aesthetic pleasure. Dorian seems to have 'forgotten all that he had gone through,' experiencing it as 'the unreality of a dream.' Wilde shows how privilege and beauty create a buffer against moral reckoning. The exquisite day, the spices, the bee buzzing around roses, all conspire to make cruelty feel like something that happened in another lifetime.

The screen hiding the portrait becomes a symbol of moral avoidance. Dorian's dread of being alone with it mirrors the universal human reluctance to confront what we have done. His fear of certainty is psychologically precise: as long as the change might be imaginary, he can maintain his innocence. The moment he locks the doors and pulls back the screen, he crosses a threshold from denial into knowledge.

Dorian's 'almost scientific interest' in the changed portrait is deeply unsettling, revealing a capacity for detachment that Lord Henry has cultivated in him. Yet the portrait also awakens conscience: he recognizes his cruelty toward Sibyl and resolves to make amends. Wilde presents the portrait as simultaneously a tool for self-knowledge and a temptation to treat one's own soul as an object of study rather than a call to change.

Lord Henry's arrival interrupts Dorian's moral awakening at the worst possible moment. Dorian's declaration that he believes in conscience now and wants to marry Sibyl represents his last real chance at redemption. Henry's stunned reaction to the marriage plan shows he recognizes, perhaps with alarm, that his protege might escape his influence. The tension builds toward the revelation that will shatter everything.

The news of Sibyl's death is a masterpiece of Wildean irony. Lord Henry delivers it with social pragmatism, worrying about scandal and inquests rather than mourning. Dorian's extraordinary response, noting that 'the roses are not less lovely' and calling her death 'a wonderful ending to a wonderful play,' marks the moment his aestheticization of life becomes genuinely monstrous. He has absorbed Henry's philosophy so deeply that he can transform a suicide into a narrative experience.

Lord Henry's long speech reframing Sibyl's death as a beautiful tragedy rather than a human catastrophe is the most seductive and dangerous passage in the novel. By comparing her death to a Greek tragedy and insisting 'the girl never really lived,' he gives Dorian permission to feel nothing. Wilde shows how aesthetic philosophy, taken to its logical conclusion, can become a machine for manufacturing indifference to suffering.

Dorian's acceptance of Henry's framework, declaring 'You have explained me to myself,' is the point of no return. He has chosen the interpretation that frees him from guilt. The evening shadows creeping into the room mark the end of his moral daylight. His wonder about whether 'life has still in store for me anything as marvellous' reveals that he now sees even tragedy as a form of aesthetic consumption.

The final scenes with the portrait complete Dorian's Faustian choice. His brief tears for Sibyl are real but quickly aestheticized into a 'wonderful tragic figure.' The decisive passage, 'Eternal youth, infinite passion, pleasures subtle and secret, wild joys and wilder sins, he was to have all these things,' is Dorian accepting the devil's bargain with full awareness. The portrait will bear his shame so he never has to.

Dorian's contemplation of whether to pray away the portrait's power, followed by his refusal, shows him choosing freedom over salvation. His reasoning that no one 'would surrender the chance of remaining always young' universalizes his moral failure. The final image of him smiling as he screens the portrait and heading to the opera captures the chilling ease with which conscience can be hidden behind a beautiful surface.
